sentence: The report ______ being written when the power went out.
explanation: Past continuous passive: <was/were being> + past participle for actions in progress in the past.
tip: The writing was in progress when something interrupted it — past continuous passive.

sentence: The new bridge ______ been completed by the end of next year.
explanation: Future perfect passive: <will have been> + past participle for actions completed before a future time.
tip: The bridge will be finished before a deadline — future perfect passive.

sentence: The documents should ______ been signed before the meeting.
explanation: Modal perfect passive: <should have been> + past participle for past obligations not fulfilled.
tip: The documents weren't signed but they should have been — modal perfect passive.

sentence: The house ______ being renovated at the moment.
explanation: Present continuous passive: <is/are being> + past participle for current ongoing actions.
tip: The renovation is happening right now — present continuous passive.

sentence: The suspect is believed ______ have left the country.
explanation: Impersonal passive with <believed>: subject + <is believed to> + have + past participle.
tip: People believe the suspect left — what word connects 'believed' to 'have left'?

sentence: She had her car ______ at the garage yesterday.
explanation: Causative <have>: <had> + object + past participle. Someone else did the repair for her.
tip: She didn't repair it herself — someone did it for her. What form of 'repair'?

sentence: I need to get my eyes ______ before I can renew my driving licence.
explanation: Causative <get>: <get> + object + past participle. Someone else will do the testing.
tip: You won't test your own eyes — someone else will. What form of 'test'?

sentence: The results ______ have been announced by now.
explanation: Modal passive for expectation: <should have been> + past participle.
tip: You expected the results to be announced already — what modal shows expectation?

sentence: The new policy is said ______ affect millions of people.
explanation: Impersonal passive with <said>: subject + <is said to> + base verb.
tip: People say the policy affects millions — what word connects 'said' to 'affect'?

sentence: She's having her hair ______ for the wedding tomorrow.
explanation: Causative <have>: <having> + object + past participle. A hairdresser will do it.
tip: A professional will style her hair — what past participle of 'do' fits?

sentence: The project might ______ been cancelled due to budget cuts.
explanation: Modal perfect passive: <might have been> + past participle for past possibility.
tip: It's possible the project was cancelled — what word completes the modal perfect?

sentence: The building ______ being demolished when we drove past.
explanation: Past continuous passive: <was being> + past participle for an action in progress at a past moment.
tip: The demolition was in progress when you saw it — past continuous passive.

sentence: He is thought ______ be one of the best scientists in the field.
explanation: Impersonal passive with <thought>: subject + <is thought to> + be.
tip: People think he is one of the best — what connects 'thought' to 'be'?

sentence: We got our house ______ last summer. It looks much better now.
explanation: Causative <get>: <got> + object + past participle. Professional painters did it.
tip: You didn't paint it yourself — what past participle shows someone else did it?

sentence: The new software ______ being tested by the IT team right now.
explanation: Present continuous passive: <is being> + past participle for current testing.
tip: The testing is happening now — present continuous passive.

sentence: The missing child is reported ______ have been seen near the park.
explanation: Impersonal passive with <reported>: subject + <is reported to> + have been + past participle.
tip: People report seeing the child — what connects 'reported' to 'have been seen'?

sentence: I must get this document ______ before the deadline.
explanation: Causative <get>: <get> + object + past participle with modal <must>.
tip: Someone else needs to sign it for you — what past participle of 'sign'?

sentence: The road ______ have been repaired months ago. It's still full of holes.
explanation: Modal perfect passive for criticism: <should have been> + past participle.
tip: The road wasn't repaired but it should have been — what modal shows this criticism?

sentence: By the time we arrived, the food had already ______ served.
explanation: Past perfect passive: <had been> + past participle for actions completed before another past event.
tip: The food was served before you arrived — what completes the past perfect passive?

sentence: She had her portrait ______ by a famous artist.
explanation: Causative <have>: <had> + object + past participle. The artist painted it for her.
tip: A famous artist painted it for her — what past participle of 'paint'?

sentence: The concert is expected ______ attract over 50,000 fans.
explanation: Impersonal passive with <expected>: subject + <is expected to> + base verb.
tip: People expect the concert to attract fans — what connects 'expected' to 'attract'?

sentence: The windows ______ being cleaned when it started to rain.
explanation: Past continuous passive: <were being> + past participle for plural subjects.
tip: The cleaning was in progress when rain started — past continuous passive (plural).

sentence: I need to have my suit ______ before the interview.
explanation: Causative <have>: <have> + object + past participle for professional services.
tip: A professional cleaner will do it — what past participle describes the service?

sentence: The new regulations could ______ been implemented more gradually.
explanation: Modal perfect passive: <could have been> + past participle for past alternative possibilities.
tip: There was a better way to implement them — what completes the modal perfect?

sentence: The company's profits are known ______ have doubled in the last quarter.
explanation: Impersonal passive with <known>: subject + <is known to> + have + past participle.
tip: People know the profits doubled — what connects 'known' to 'have doubled'?
